findings.finding: Cross-cancel active-runner check (Task 7) uses ipc.read_runner_pid + verify_runner_pid only; during the 5s handshake window runner.pid does not exist, so cortex overnight schedule can be issued against a session in phase:starting.
findings.evidence_quote: for the active-runner check, ipc.read_runner_pid() is at cortex_command/overnight/ipc.py:381 and verify_runner_pid() at :392. Use the existing helpers as-is.

findings.finding: Two distinct deleters of runner.spawn-pending (parent handle_start in run-now path; runner subprocess in launchd path) with no ordering specification relative to runner.pid visibility — a status query in the gap can observe pid-absent + sentinel-absent.
findings.evidence_quote: the spawn-pending sentinel is written by the launcher, and the launchd-spawned runner deletes it after writing runner.pid (covered by the existing runner code path; no change here)

findings.finding: spawn.py wait_for_pid_file specifies a 5.0s timeout but no poll interval or backoff; contract is open to busy-wait or coarse polling that makes the happy-path test flaky.
findings.evidence_quote: spawn.py exposes wait_for_pid_file(path: Path, timeout: float = 5.0) -> int | None (returns pid on success, None on timeout).

findings.finding: tests/test_runner_pr_gating.py runs 11 tests that invoke cortex overnight start --dry-run via subprocess.run with timeout=120 and parse result.stdout for DRY-RUN gh pr create lines. Plan Task 6 does not state whether the dry-run path stays inline or takes the new async-spawn fork path; if folded into async-spawn the 11 tests silently lose their assertion target.
findings.evidence_quote: subprocess.run([cortex, overnight, start, --dry-run, --state, str(state_path)], env=env, capture_output=True, text=True, stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL, timeout=120,)

findings.finding: tests/test_runner_signal.py:179 and tests/test_runner_followup_commit.py:206 send SIGHUP to proc.pid expecting it to be the runner. Under Task 6 start_new_session=True async-spawn, proc.pid is the shim that already returned; signals do not reach the reparented runner. These suites break irrecoverably and Task 6 verification does not include them.
findings.evidence_quote: (2) fork the runner via subprocess.Popen with start_new_session=True + stdin=DEVNULL + redirected stdout/stderr to session log files

findings.finding: justfile:75 overnight-run recipe invokes cortex overnight start synchronously; README.md:125 documents just overnight-run as Run overnight in foreground. After Task 6 the recipe returns within 5s and the runner detaches — foreground becomes false. Task 10 does not touch the justfile recipe or README.
findings.evidence_quote: cortex overnight start --state {{ state }} --time-limit {{ time-limit }} --max-rounds {{ max-rounds }} --tier {{ tier }}

findings.finding: tests/test_cli_overnight_format_json.py:245 calls cli_handler.handle_start synchronously with format=json and a pre-written live runner.pid, then reads captured stdout for the concurrent_runner JSON envelope. The test depends on the pre-flight JSON refusal at cli_handler.py:269-288 firing BEFORE the new async-spawn step; Task 6 description does not pin the ordering.
findings.evidence_quote: Identify the shell-out point and split into _spawn_runner_async() (new, returns immediately after handshake) and _run_runner_inline() (existing-blocking-path renamed; only invoked under --launchd).
